[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [50°06′39″N 75°23′30″W﻿ / ﻿50.1107°N 75.3918°W﻿ / 50.1107; -75.3918](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=New_Britain_(Canada)&params=50.1107_N_75.3918_W_)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Historical region of Canada

***New Britain*** as a historical term of limited usage referred in its day to the poorly mapped lands of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") north of 17th-century [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"). The name applied primarily to today's [Nunavik](/wiki/Nunavik "Nunavik") and [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador") interiors, though in the 18th century this had grown to include all of the mainland shores of [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") and [James Bay](/wiki/James_Bay "James Bay") north of [the Canadas](/wiki/The_Canadas "The Canadas"). British visitors came to sub-divide the district loosely into the territories of **New South Wales**, **New North Wales** and **Labrador**. The name *Labrador* predates mention of the other names by more than a century.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Early exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Britain_(Canada)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early exploration")]

In 1612, [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people") captain [Thomas Button](/wiki/Thomas_Button "Thomas Button") wintered on the shores of Hudson Bay, at the mouth of the river he named the [Nelson](/wiki/Nelson_River "Nelson River"). He dubbed his encampment [Port Nelson](/wiki/Port_Nelson,_Manitoba "Port Nelson, Manitoba"), and "the whole of the western shore New Wales."[[2]](#cite_note-2) Seven years later, in 1619, [Danish](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") captain [Jens Munk](/wiki/Jens_Munk "Jens Munk") wintered nearby at the mouth of the [Churchill River](/wiki/Churchill_River_(Hudson_Bay) "Churchill River (Hudson Bay)"), naming those environs *Nova Dania*[[3]](#cite_note-3) (Latin for "New Denmark").

The region was again visited twelve years later in 1631 by Captains [Thomas James](/wiki/Thomas_James_(sea_captain) "Thomas James (sea captain)") and [Luke Foxe](/wiki/Luke_Foxe "Luke Foxe"). Supposedly, Captain Foxe, upon discovering a cross erected by Button at Port Nelson, christened the shore north of the Nelson River as *New North Wales*, and all the lands south as *New South Wales*.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Another account attributes the event to Captain James, while crediting Foxe with having bestowed upon the region the since-forgotten label of *New Yorkshire.*[[5]](#cite_note-5)

* New North Wales – Mainland [Kivalliq](/wiki/Kivalliq_Region "Kivalliq Region") in Nunavut, and the [Northern Region](/wiki/Northern_Region,_Manitoba "Northern Region, Manitoba") in [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") south to Port Nelson.
* New South Wales – Northern Manitoba south from Port Nelson to [James Bay](/wiki/James_Bay "James Bay"), including the [Kenora District](/wiki/Kenora_District,_Ontario "Kenora District, Ontario") in [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario").
* Labrador – The eastern coast of Hudson Bay, including [Nord-du-Québec](/wiki/Nord-du-Qu%C3%A9bec "Nord-du-Québec") in [Québec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") and modern [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador") in [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador"). During the "New Britain" era, the only European settlements in Labrador were the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") missions at [Nain](/wiki/Nain,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Nain, Newfoundland and Labrador") (1771), [Okak](/wiki/Okak,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Okak, Newfoundland and Labrador") (1776), and [Hopedale](/wiki/Hopedale,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Hopedale, Newfoundland and Labrador") (1782).

139 years later, Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") more successfully used the name *New South Wales* for the Australian [Colony of New South Wales](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales") which eventually encompassed most of [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_(Australia) "New Holland (Australia)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) By this time, the North American name had begun to fall into obscurity.
